title: New approaches to almost i.i.d. information theory
authors: Filippo Girardi, Giacomo De Palma, Ludovico Lami
abstract: Independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) states are ubiquitous in quantum information theory. However, in a practical setting, the i.i.d. assumption is too stringent, and possibly not realistic. A physically more compelling class of 'almost i.i.d.' sources was recently proposed by [Mazzola/Sutter/Renner, arXiv:2603.15792]. In this paper, we introduce two alternative definitions of almost i.i.d. states, based on the normalised quantum Wasserstein distance and on the idea of looking at the average $k$-body marginal. We explore some basic properties of these notions and prove a strict hierarchical relation among them, with Mazzola et al.'s notion being the strictest, the one based on $k$-body marginals the loosest, and the one based on the quantum Wasserstein distance in between. Strict separation is established by means of explicit examples.
summary: The paper introduces new mathematical frameworks for 'almost i.i.d.' quantum sources to better model real-world systems with local correlations. It proves that these new definitions form a strict hierarchy, ranging from the highly constrained MSR model to a looser definition based on average k-body marginals.
main_problem: The standard i.i.d. assumption in quantum information theory is often too restrictive for practical, physical settings. The paper seeks to formalize and compare alternative mathematical definitions of 'almost i.i.d.' sources that better represent real-world devices with memory or local perturbations.
main_result: The authors establish a strict hierarchical relation between three notions of almost i.i.d. states: MSR almost i.i.d. is the strictest, followed by Wasserstein almost i.i.d., with weakly almost i.i.d. being the loosest. They provide explicit counter-examples to prove that these inclusions are strict.
method: The study utilizes quantum Wasserstein distance (order 1), trace distance, and analysis of k-body marginals. Mathematical tools include the symmetric group, type theory, and multilinear polynomials to establish bounds and separations.
model_or_system: The paper considers n-partite quantum states (and classical probability distributions) acting on a Hilbert space H^n, specifically investigating sequences of states that deviate from the ideal i.i.d. structure.
key_observables: von Neumann entropy, conditional entropy, squashed entanglement, k-body marginals, and quantum Wasserstein distance.
important_parameters: n (number of subsystems), r (number of defects), k (size of local marginals), and the scaling regime where r = o(n).
main_assumptions: The initial definitions and notation assume a finite-dimensional Hilbert space.
paper_structure: The paper begins by introducing two new definitions of almost i.i.d. states (Wasserstein and weakly almost i.i.d.) relative to the MSR definition. It then explores their properties, proves the hierarchical inclusion of the three classes, and concludes with explicit constructions of counter-examples to demonstrate the strictness of the hierarchy.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics, as it provides a more realistic mathematical framework for studying quantum processes where the assumption of independent, memoryless noise or sources is physically unrealistic.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A pictorial representation of the hierarchical relation between the various notions of almost i.i.d.-ness discussed in this paper: the Mazzola–Sutter–Renner notion is the strictest, the one derived from Wasserstein distance is the intermediate one, and, finally, weak almost i.i.d.-ness is the broadest. All inclusions are strict.
